title: Personalizing Dialogue Agents: I have a dog, do you have pets too?
venue: Proceedings of the 56th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: Does conditioning a dialogue model on a short persona profile make its conversation more engaging, consistent, and recognizably tied to that profile?
method: The authors introduced Persona-Chat, trained retrieval and generative dialogue models with and without persona conditioning, and compared them using automatic metrics plus blinded human ratings of fluency, engagement, consistency, and persona identification.
population_or_data: 10,981 crowdsourced dialogues containing 164,356 utterances, built from 1,155 fictional personas; model-level human evaluation used 100 conversations per model.
core_finding: Models trained on Persona-Chat were more engaging than Twitter- and OpenSubtitles-trained baselines. Within Persona-Chat, own-persona conditioning made assigned profiles more detectable and improved consistency, but did not improve engagingness; human ratings had high variance. Revised persona descriptions were harder to identify, and interlocutor-persona effects were weaker and instruction-sensitive.
architecture_implications: Evaluate a digital brain with blinded owner and non-owner judgments that separately score consistency, engagement, and identity recognizability instead of collapsing them into one resemblance score.
Include paraphrased and minimally stated profile variants so a system cannot pass by repeating canonical profile sentences.
Report rater dispersion and persona-detection confusion, not only model-level means.
limitations: The personas were short fictional profiles enacted by crowdworkers in brief getting-to-know-you conversations, not longitudinal records of real people. The 100-dialogue model samples and high rater variance limit precision, and profile detectability is not evidence of individual predictive validity.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org PersonaChat personalizing dialogue agents human evaluation consistency
"Personalizing Dialogue Agents" P18-1205

title: Dialogue Natural Language Inference
venue: Proceedings of the 57th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics
research_question: Can persona consistency in dialogue be operationalized as natural-language entailment and contradiction, and can that signal reduce contradictory model responses?
method: The study constructed Dialogue NLI from Persona-Chat persona statements and dialogue utterances, trained entailment classifiers, and used them to rerank candidate responses before automatic and human evaluation.
population_or_data: Persona-Chat profiles and utterances converted into entailment, neutral, and contradiction pairs, with in-domain and out-of-domain NLI models evaluated on the resulting benchmark.
core_finding: A classifier trained on Dialogue NLI recognized persona entailment and contradiction far better than an SNLI-only model, and NLI-based reranking reduced contradictory responses in human and automatic evaluations.
architecture_implications: Maintain an explicit contradiction test between generated claims and time-valid owner evidence before evaluating stylistic resemblance.
Train and validate consistency detectors on conversational evidence from the target domain; generic NLI transfer is insufficient.
Keep entailment, neutrality, and contradiction outputs visible because absence of contradiction does not establish faithful identity.
limitations: The benchmark centers on explicit factual statements in synthetic Persona-Chat profiles. It does not test tacit preferences, style, values, relationship-specific behavior, temporal change, or whether a response predicts what a real owner would do.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org "Dialogue Natural Language Inference" persona consistency
P19-1363 Dialogue NLI primary paper

title: InCharacter: Evaluating Personality Fidelity in Role-Playing Agents through Psychological Interviews
venue: Proceedings of the 62nd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: Can open-ended psychological interviews measure whether role-playing agents express the intended character personality more faithfully than direct questionnaire prompting?
method: InCharacter interviewed role-playing agents with questions derived from 14 psychological scales, converted free-form answers to scale choices using an assessor model, and compared inferred traits with character labels and repeated runs.
population_or_data: 32 fictional characters, 14 personality scales, and 18,304 interview dialogues; reference labels came from human annotations and the Personality Database depending on the scale.
core_finding: Interview-based assessment aligned better with character labels than direct self-report prompting and showed low score dispersion across three runs; the best reported dimensional alignment was 80.7 percent against Personality Database 16P labels.
architecture_implications: Evaluate latent traits through open-ended, behaviorally grounded interviews as well as direct questionnaires, because forced-choice self-description can distort a role-playing model's profile.
Repeat each psychometric interview across seeds and report score dispersion before treating a trait estimate as stable.
Separate the interviewer, assessor, and target-agent roles and audit each component so evaluator errors are not attributed to the persona.
limitations: The pipeline depends on an LLM interviewer and assessor that can introduce their own bias and errors. Ground truth is a single static label for each fictional character even when narratives imply personality change, and fictional-character agreement is not owner validation.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org InCharacter personality fidelity psychological interviews
"InCharacter" 2024 ACL role-playing agents

title: CharacterEval: A Chinese Benchmark for Role-Playing Conversational Agent Evaluation
venue: Proceedings of the 62nd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: How can role-playing dialogue be evaluated across character knowledge, personality, behavior, and linguistic style with judgments that track humans?
method: The authors built multi-turn character dialogues, defined 13 criteria across four dimensions, collected human ratings, and trained CharacterRM to predict the ratings while comparing it with general-purpose LLM judges.
population_or_data: 1,785 multi-turn dialogues and 11,376 evaluated examples covering 77 characters from Chinese novels and scripts, annotated by 12 human judges.
core_finding: Roleplay-specific evaluation captured dimensions missed by generic judges. CharacterRM achieved substantially higher overall correlation with human ratings than the tested GPT-4 judging setups, and specialized role-playing models often outperformed GPT-4 on character fidelity.
architecture_implications: Use a multidimensional scorecard that keeps factual knowledge, personality, behavior, and linguistic style separate.
Calibrate automated judges against blinded human ratings on the intended language and cultural domain before using them for model selection.
Preserve turn-level rationales and dimension scores so a strong style score cannot hide knowledge or behavioral failures.
limitations: The benchmark is limited to Chinese literary and scripted characters and depends on subjective judgments from a small annotator pool. Its MBTI back-test does not establish broad psychometric validity, and literary role fidelity does not equal fidelity to a living owner.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org CharacterEval Chinese role-playing conversational agent evaluation
"CharacterEval" ACL 2024 human correlation

title: TimeChara: Evaluating Point-in-Time Character Hallucination of Role-Playing Large Language Models
venue: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2024
research_question: Do role-playing language models respect what a character knew and how the character behaved at a specified point in a narrative timeline?
method: TimeChara generated temporal character questions from long-form fiction, labeled answers by whether information was available at the target point, evaluated current models primarily with GPT-4 judgments, validated that judge against human ratings on a 50-instance subset, and tested a Narrative-Experts prompting method.
population_or_data: 10,895 instances drawn from four English novel series; the main model comparison used 600 sampled instances, while evaluator validation used 50 of those instances and 27 qualified human annotators.
core_finding: State-of-the-art models, including strong proprietary systems, frequently used future or otherwise unavailable information when answering as a past version of a character. Narrative-Experts reduced these point-in-time hallucinations but did not eliminate them.
architecture_implications: Version owner memory and persona state by observation time, and evaluate responses against the information available at the requested cutoff rather than against an all-time profile.
Create counterfactual probes that distinguish known-at-time, not-yet-known, and never-known information.
Track temporal leakage as its own failure class even when the generated statement is globally true.
limitations: The source material is four English book series rather than real longitudinal lives. Automated judging adds model bias and cost, the questions are structurally constrained, and the proposed mitigation adds latency while remaining incomplete.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org TimeChara point-in-time character hallucination
"TimeChara" temporal role-playing LLM evaluation

alternate_ids.value: SOTOPIA: Interactive Evaluation for Social Intelligence in Language Agents (2024)

title: SOTOPIA: Interactive Evaluation for Social Intelligence in Language Agents
venue: The Twelfth International Conference on Learning Representations
research_question: How well do language agents pursue goals and display social intelligence during interactive role-play across relationships and scenarios?
method: SOTOPIA paired human or language-model agents in generated dyadic social scenarios, scored interactions on seven social dimensions, and compared an LLM evaluator with human judgments on a harder subset.
population_or_data: Dyadic text role-plays spanning five relationship types and varied social goals, with human-agent and agent-agent interactions plus human annotations for evaluator validation.
core_finding: Strong language models remained below humans on difficult social interactions, especially where social commonsense and strategic communication mattered. The GPT-4 evaluator tracked human goal ratings better than several social dimensions, where agreement was materially weaker.
architecture_implications: Test a digital brain in interactive, relationship-conditioned scenarios rather than only scoring isolated replies.
Score goal completion separately from believability, rapport, norm compliance, and knowledge of the other person.
Require human calibration for each social dimension because an automated judge's validity is not uniform across constructs.
limitations: The benchmark uses short, dyadic, fixed-turn episodes, five relationship categories, and no persistent identity across sessions. Automated evaluation can reproduce stereotypes or evaluator bias, and scenario success is not proof of a specific person's behavior.

search_queries: site:proceedings.iclr.cc SOTOPIA interactive evaluation social intelligence language agents
SOTOPIA human evaluation relationship role-play ICLR 2024

title: Evaluating Large Language Models as Generative User Simulators for Conversational Recommendation
venue: Proceedings of the 2024 Conference of the North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics: Human Language Technologies (Volume 1: Long Papers)
research_question: Do LLM-generated users reproduce the preferences, requests, and feedback patterns needed to evaluate conversational recommender systems?
method: The study defined five user-simulation tasks, compared LLM outputs with human data from four real-world movie datasets, and analyzed preference agreement, popularity bias, request specificity, and feedback coherence under different prompts and models.
population_or_data: Human movie-preference and conversational-recommendation data from ReDial, Reddit, IMDb, and MovieLens, evaluated through item choice, preference expression, request generation, and recommendation feedback tasks.
core_finding: The tested LLM simulators showed popularity bias, weak agreement with human preferences, generic requests, and feedback that could conflict with stated preferences. Prompting and model choice improved some metrics but did not make the simulators faithful human replacements.
architecture_implications: Validate a user simulator against held-out human choices and preference-conditioned feedback before using it to optimize a dialogue policy.
Measure popularity bias, preference consistency, request specificity, and feedback coherence separately.
Treat synthetic-user evaluation as necessary but insufficient; preserve a real-user acceptance test for policy changes.
limitations: The experiments focus on movies and mostly static recommendation tasks. They do not cover evolving tastes, relationship history, or consequential real-world behavior, and benchmark overlap with model training data cannot be fully ruled out.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org evaluating large language models generative user simulators conversational recommendation
2024.naacl-long.83 user simulator human preference

title: Neural User Simulation for Corpus-based Policy Optimisation of Spoken Dialogue Systems
venue: Proceedings of the 19th Annual SIGdial Meeting on Discourse and Dialogue
research_question: Can a corpus-trained neural user simulator produce dialogue policies that transfer to real users better than policies trained against an agenda-based simulator?
method: A sequence-to-sequence simulator was trained on DSTC2 dialogues, used as the environment for reinforcement-learning policy optimization, cross-tested against another simulator, and finally evaluated through 1,000 dialogues with human users.
population_or_data: DSTC2 restaurant-information dialogues plus 1,000 Mechanical Turk conversations, 250 for each evaluated policy.
core_finding: The neural-simulator-trained policy achieved the highest reported success with real users, but extended optimization also revealed overfitting to simulator-specific behavior. Cross-simulator success alone did not fully predict human performance.
architecture_implications: Judge a user simulator by downstream transfer to held-out real interactions, not by superficial utterance realism alone.
Use multiple simulators and monitor divergence between simulated reward and real-user outcomes during policy optimization.
Stop or regularize training when simulator reward rises while cross-simulator or human validation stalls.
limitations: The domain is narrow, slot-based restaurant search with task-oriented crowdworkers. Policy success is an indirect fidelity measure rather than person-level resemblance, and the observed simulator exploitation warns against extrapolating to open-ended identity dialogue.

alternate_ids.value: Using Large Language Models to Simulate Multiple Humans and Replicate Human Subject Studies (2023)

title: Using Large Language Models to Simulate Multiple Humans and Replicate Human Subject Studies
venue: Proceedings of the 40th International Conference on Machine Learning
research_question: Can prompted language models simulate participant populations closely enough to reproduce findings from classic human-subject experiments?
method: The authors ran Turing Experiments that prompted multiple GPT-family models as participants in the ultimatum game, garden-path interpretation, Milgram obedience, and wisdom-of-crowds studies, then compared aggregate patterns with the original human results.
population_or_data: Four classic experimental paradigms evaluated across multiple GPT-3-era and instruction-tuned language models with demographic or role prompts depending on the study.
core_finding: The models reproduced several qualitative experimental effects, but the wisdom-of-crowds simulation collapsed toward identical, unusually accurate answers rather than human-like error diversity. Model and prompt choices materially affected replication.
architecture_implications: Require fidelity tests for both means and distributions; matching an average while collapsing variance is a failure for a digital brain.
Include adversarially novel variants and contamination checks when evaluating famous tasks that may appear in pretraining data.
Measure within-person coherence separately from across-population diversity so a single language-model prior is not mistaken for many individuals.
limitations: Only four well-known studies and a related family of GPT models were tested. Training-data exposure to the paradigms cannot be excluded, demographic prompting is an incomplete identity intervention, and replicated aggregate effects do not establish individual prediction.

search_queries: site:proceedings.mlr.press Turing Experiments simulate multiple humans replicate human subject studies
"Using Large Language Models to Simulate Multiple Humans" 2208.10264

title: Out of One, Many: Using Language Models to Simulate Human Samples
venue: Political Analysis
research_question: Can a language model conditioned on survey respondents' demographic and attitudinal backstories reproduce response distributions and subgroup patterns in human political samples?
method: GPT-3 was conditioned on thousands of real survey respondents' backstories and asked to generate answers, which were compared with the respondents' recorded political responses at individual and aggregate levels under the authors' algorithmic-fidelity framework.
population_or_data: Large United States political-survey samples represented through textual backstories assembled from respondent attributes and prior survey answers.
core_finding: Conditioned GPT-3 outputs reproduced several aggregate and subgroup response patterns in the studied surveys, supporting the possibility of structured synthetic samples while still leaving person-level and distributional validity unresolved.
architecture_implications: Benchmark profile-conditioned predictions against unconditioned and demographics-only baselines to quantify the incremental value of personal evidence.
Report individual agreement, subgroup calibration, covariance, and full response distributions rather than only aggregate similarity.
Pre-register held-out questions so profile fields cannot directly encode the target answer.
limitations: The evidence is confined to United States political surveys and GPT-3. Aggregate or subgroup correspondence can conceal compressed variance and individual errors, backstory fields may closely cue outcomes, and the abstract-level inspection here does not support stronger causal claims.

alternate_ids.value: Synthetic Replacements for Human Survey Data? The Perils of Large Language Models (2024)

title: Synthetic Replacements for Human Survey Data? The Perils of Large Language Models
venue: Political Analysis
research_question: Do persona-conditioned LLM survey responses preserve the variance, relationships, subgroup differences, and temporal stability of the human data they are intended to replace?
method: The study prompted ChatGPT to answer as matched ANES respondents, compared synthetic and human affective-polarization outcomes across descriptive and regression analyses, tested alternative prompts and models, and repeated prompts after the deployed model changed.
population_or_data: 2016 and 2020 American National Election Studies respondents and 11 sociopolitical groups, with supplemental replications using GPT-4 and Falcon.
core_finding: Synthetic means could look similar to human means while response variance was too small, regression relationships and subgroup hostility patterns differed, and results were sensitive to prompt wording and to a model update over a three-month interval.
architecture_implications: Set variance, covariance, subgroup error, and temporal reproducibility gates in addition to mean agreement.
Pin model versions and preserve prompts, decoding settings, and evaluation dates so behavioral drift can be detected rather than silently absorbed.
Use repeated real-person outcomes as the primary benchmark; persona prompting alone is not a synthetic-data validation method.
limitations: The main application is United States political affect with one initially tested closed model, and model behavior is entangled with changing alignment policies. The study critiques synthetic replacement more directly than individualized long-term agents, but the distributional and temporal failures transfer to that setting.

alternate_ids.value: LLM Agents Grounded in Self-Reports Enable General-Purpose Simulation of Individuals (2024)

title: LLM Agents Grounded in Self-Reports Enable General-Purpose Simulation of Individuals
venue: arXiv preprint arXiv:2411.10109 (v3 revised 2026)
research_question: How accurately can interview-grounded LLM agents reproduce an individual's held-out survey answers, personality measures, economic-game behavior, and experimental responses relative to that person's own retest reliability?
method: The authors recruited participants for two-hour semistructured voice interviews, built agents from interview transcripts and survey responses, and compared agent predictions with participants' answers across held-out General Social Survey items, Big Five scales, economic games, and experiments, using a two-week human retest as an upper-reference measure.
population_or_data: 1,052 United States adults; interviews averaged 6,491 words and were paired with surveys, Big Five measures, five economic games, five experiments, and a two-week retest.
core_finding: Interview-grounded agents reached 83 percent of participants' own test-retest agreement on held-out GSS items, versus 74 percent for demographics-only agents; adding survey data raised this to 86 percent. The advantage did not extend clearly to economic games, and the five-experiment analysis was underpowered.
architecture_implications: Normalize prediction scores by each owner's test-retest reliability so model error is compared with the person's own temporal instability.
Hold out entire instruments and behaviors from profile construction, and compare interview, demographics-only, survey-only, and combined evidence ablations.
Keep behavioral domains separate because survey gains cannot be assumed to transfer to games or experimental effects.
limitations: This is a preprint using one United States English-speaking sample and one proprietary model family. Economic-game predictions did not beat simple baselines, experiment-level evidence was underpowered, and individual item agreement does not guarantee correct population covariance. Rich interviews also create consent, withdrawal, and re-identification risks.

alternate_ids.value: Large Language Models Assume People are More Rational than We Really are (2025)

title: Large Language Models Assume People are More Rational than We Really are
venue: The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations
research_question: Do LLM predictions of human risky choice reproduce actual behavioral noise and biases, or do they substitute a more rational model of decision-making?
method: Several proprietary and open LLMs predicted choices on the Choices13k risky-decision benchmark under direct, population, and chain-of-thought prompts; predictions were compared with repeated human choices and expected-value rationality.
population_or_data: Choices13k, containing 13,006 risky-choice problems; the principal subset contained 9,831 problems with at least 15 participants and repeated decisions.
core_finding: Models could track human judgments while still predicting behavior as too expected-value rational. Chain-of-thought increased this mismatch; for one reported comparison, GPT-4o's choices correlated far more strongly with expected value than human choices did.
architecture_implications: Evaluate against realized owner behavior, not an evaluator's judgment that a prediction sounds psychologically plausible.
Include stochastic repeated-choice tests and measure excess rationality, variance, and probability calibration.
Treat chain-of-thought and other prompt changes as evaluation factors because they can move a simulator away from human behavior.
limitations: The benchmark consists of abstract risky choices rather than rich personal decisions, participants are represented without longitudinal profiles, and results depend on prompt and model versions. It tests population behavioral realism more directly than owner-specific fidelity.

search_queries: site:proceedings.iclr.cc "Large Language Models Assume People are More Rational"
Choices13k LLM human behavior expected value rationality ICLR 2025

title: Evaluating the ability of large language models to emulate personality
venue: Scientific Reports
research_question: Can GPT-4 emulate real Big Five profiles while preserving psychometric reliability, factor structure, and criterion validity under profiles of different complexity?
method: Two simulations prompted GPT-4 to role-play profiles derived from real respondents and complete the 50-item IPIP Big Five measure; the authors compared convergence, internal consistency, factor loadings, intertrait structure, and criterion validity under trait-only and trait-plus-demographic profiles.
population_or_data: Two sets of 400 real-person profiles used to condition simulated IPIP-BFM-50 responses, with increasingly complex profile information in the second simulation.
core_finding: Emulated scores converged strongly with target traits and were internally consistent, but factor loadings were implausibly high relative to human data and human-like intertrait dependencies were weakened. Criterion validity and robustness changed when demographic complexity was added.
architecture_implications: Require convergent, discriminant, factorial, and criterion validity together; high target-score correlation alone can reward unnaturally pure trait caricatures.
Compare human and agent item covariance and cross-trait dependencies, not just scale totals.
Stress-test psychometrics as profile complexity grows because adding context can change both means and validity.
limitations: The evidence comes from questionnaire role-play rather than observed behavior and focuses on GPT-4 and one Big Five instrument. Profile conditioning may directly cue desired traits, and simulated internal consistency can be high precisely because responses are less humanly heterogeneous.

search_queries: site:nature.com/articles evaluating ability large language models emulate personality psychometric
10.1038/s41598-024-84109-5 factor loadings GPT-4

title: Personality testing of large language models: limited temporal stability, but highlighted prosociality
venue: Royal Society Open Science
research_question: Are personality-test profiles elicited from LLMs stable across repeated measurements, and how do their trait patterns compare with human norms?
method: Seven LLMs completed multiple personality instruments on two occasions five days apart; the authors analyzed mean profiles and test-retest stability using intraclass correlations.
population_or_data: Seven proprietary and open LLMs tested with established personality questionnaires in 2022 or 2024, with each model-instrument combination repeated after five days.
core_finding: Models tended to produce socially desirable, prosocial profiles, while test-retest stability varied substantially by model, trait, and instrument. Some newer models were more stable, but high stability was not a general property of LLM personality scores.
architecture_implications: Run identical owner-model psychometrics across dates, sessions, and deployment versions and report trait-level reliability.
Distinguish desirable alignment behavior from owner fidelity; prosocial responding can systematically bias personality estimates.
Version every score by model, prompt, instrument, and date so platform drift is observable.
limitations: Only two occasions five days apart were measured, prompts asked models to act as humans, and platform/model differences are confounded with collection date. Stability of questionnaire output does not establish stability of relationship behavior or person-specific validity.

search_queries: PubMed personality testing large language models limited temporal stability prosociality
10.1098/rsos.240180 test retest LLM personality

title: Reducing Conversational Agents' Overconfidence Through Linguistic Calibration
venue: Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics
research_question: Can a conversational agent express verbal confidence that corresponds to the probability that its factual answer is correct?
method: The authors collected human correctness and linguistic-confidence labels for knowledge-grounded chatbot answers, measured calibration of BlenderBot-style systems, trained a correctness calibrator, and controlled response generation to better match expressed confidence to correctness.
population_or_data: English conversational answers to TriviaQA-derived factual questions produced by BlenderBot and BST variants, with human annotations of answer correctness and confidence language.
core_finding: The baseline conversational agents were poorly linguistically calibrated. A learned correctness predictor and controlled generation improved alignment between verbal confidence and actual correctness without reducing answer accuracy in the tested setting.
architecture_implications: Evaluate whether confidence wording is calibrated to held-out evidence correctness, not merely whether uncertainty phrases appear.
Give the generator an explicit confidence signal tied to evidence quality and test calibration curves by claim type.
Create a separate calibration benchmark for owner memories, inferred preferences, and future-behavior predictions because these have different uncertainty sources.
limitations: The task is English factual question answering inside conversation, not subjective identity or autobiographical recall. The method uses internal representations and retraining, baseline factual accuracy is limited, and transfer to owner-specific uncertainty is untested.

search_queries: site:aclanthology.org linguistic calibration conversational agents overconfidence
2022.tacl-1.50 correctness confidence dialogue

alternate_ids.value: Extracting Training Data from Large Language Models (2021)

title: Extracting Training Data from Large Language Models
venue: 30th USENIX Security Symposium
research_question: Can an attacker recover verbatim training examples, including personally identifying text, from a large generative language model using only model queries?
method: The authors generated many GPT-2 samples with several sampling schemes, ranked likely memorized outputs using six membership-style metrics, manually searched candidate text, and confirmed matches against the original training corpus with the model provider.
population_or_data: GPT-2 model sizes trained on WebText; 200,000 generations per sampling strategy in the main extraction pipeline, with manual inspection of ranked candidates and corpus confirmation.
core_finding: The black-box attack recovered hundreds of verbatim training sequences, including names, contact details, chat logs, code, and identifiers. Some extracted sequences appeared in only one training document, and larger GPT-2 variants were more vulnerable.
architecture_implications: Make adversarial training-data extraction a release gate for any model trained or fine-tuned on personal communications.
Test both unprompted sampling and context-seeded attacks, and verify candidate leaks against an access-controlled provenance index.
Treat verbatim private-text emission as a fidelity failure even when it makes an answer appear more personally accurate.
limitations: The attack targets GPT-2/WebText and establishes a lower bound rather than total memorization. Candidate confirmation required access to or cooperation around the training corpus, manual web search can miss nonpublic text, and the tested model predates modern alignment layers.

USENIX Security 2021 Carlini GPT-2 training data extraction

alternate_ids.value: Quantifying Memorization Across Neural Language Models (2023)

title: Quantifying Memorization Across Neural Language Models
venue: The Eleventh International Conference on Learning Representations
research_question: How does extractable verbatim memorization vary with model capacity, training-example duplication, prompt context length, and model family?
method: Using models with known training corpora, the study prompted models with training prefixes and checked exact 50-token continuations across uniform and duplication-normalized samples, model sizes, context lengths, and model families.
population_or_data: GPT-Neo models from 125 million to 6 billion parameters trained on the Pile, with additional GPT-2 and OPT family comparisons and hundreds of thousands of sampled training sequences.
core_finding: Within model families, extractable memorization increased approximately log-linearly with model size, example duplication, and available context. Larger GPT-Neo models memorized two to five times more than smaller variants, while cross-family differences complicated simple scaling predictions.
architecture_implications: Stratify leakage tests by message duplication, context length, and model scale rather than reporting one aggregate extraction rate.
Deduplicate repeated personal messages before any training use and test long-context prompts that may unlock otherwise hidden sequences.
Maintain model-family-specific privacy baselines because parameter count alone does not predict memorization across architectures and datasets.
limitations: The operational definition requires exact greedy continuation after a true training prefix and therefore measures discoverable worst-case memorization, not all semantic leakage or an attacker's practical knowledge. Estimates use samples rather than exhaustive corpora and mostly public base-model families.

search_queries: site:openreview.net "Quantifying Memorization Across Neural Language Models"
arxiv 2202.07646 model size duplication context memorization

title: Analyzing Leakage of Personally Identifiable Information in Language Models
venue: 2023 IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy
research_question: How much personally identifiable information can black-box attackers extract, reconstruct, or infer from fine-tuned language models, and how well do scrubbing and differential privacy prevent it?
method: The paper formalized three game-based PII leakage threats, attacked GPT-2 variants fine-tuned on legal, health-care, and email corpora, and compared undefended, scrubbed, differentially private, and combined training pipelines.
population_or_data: GPT-2 models of multiple sizes fine-tuned on European Court of Human Rights cases, health-care reviews, and Enron emails, with PII identified through off-the-shelf NER tools.
core_finding: New black-box attacks recovered up to ten times more PII than prior approaches. Differential privacy substantially reduced leakage but did not eliminate it, while aggressive scrubbing and combined defenses traded privacy gains against model utility.
architecture_implications: Evaluate extraction, reconstruction from partial context, and candidate inference as separate privacy threat models.
Measure privacy and utility jointly for redaction, deduplication, and differential-privacy configurations; no single defense should be assumed complete.
Include human-audited PII and quasi-identifier benchmarks because NER-only scoring misses contextual and compositional identity leakage.
limitations: The experiments use fine-tuned GPT-2 variants and only three domains. The same imperfect NER tooling contributes to scrubbing and measurement, so reported leakage is a lower bound; exact-token definitions also miss semantic or linkability attacks.

search_queries: arxiv 2302.00539 personally identifiable information leakage language models
10.1109/SP46215.2023.10179300 PII extraction inference reconstruction

alternate_ids.value: Scalable Extraction of Training Data from Aligned, Production Language Models (2025)

title: Scalable Extraction of Training Data from Aligned, Production Language Models
venue: The Thirteenth International Conference on Learning Representations
research_question: Can alignment and conversational guardrails prevent scalable extraction of memorized training text from deployed production language models?
method: The authors evaluated memorization across open, semi-closed, and production models, built auxiliary web-scale corpora for match verification, and developed two attacks that make aligned chat models behave more like base models and emit training data.
population_or_data: Multiple open and API-served language-model families, including the 2023-era gpt-3.5-turbo and gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct endpoints; baseline probing of ChatGPT generated 50 million tokens, and candidate outputs were matched against a nine-terabyte auxiliary corpus.
core_finding: Alignment made standard extraction probes appear much less successful, but targeted attacks recovered more than ten thousand training examples from the tested ChatGPT deployment at roughly 200 dollars in query cost. The attack raised observed training-data emission dramatically over prior probing.
architecture_implications: Do not treat refusals or low leakage under ordinary prompts as evidence of privacy; red-team the boundary between aligned chat behavior and base-model behavior.
Repeat extraction audits after every model or alignment update because the demonstrated production attack was endpoint-specific.
Track unique leaked sequences and sensitive-content severity in addition to the fraction of generated tokens that match training data.
limitations: The production-model attack was specific to model versions tested in 2023 and the proprietary training corpus was unavailable, so web-corpus matching yields a lower bound and cannot prove every match was in training. Attack effectiveness, endpoints, and safeguards can change over time.

search_queries: site:openreview.net "Scalable Extraction of Training Data from Aligned, Production Language Models"
arxiv 2311.17035 ChatGPT training data extraction alignment
